title: In the future of 2015 well all have laptops and cellphones
display_title: In the future of 2015 well all have laptops and cellphones
wikitext:
[]: In the future of 2015, we'll all have laptops and cellphones             Quote Post by V » Sat Dec 06, 2008 11:08 am I'm very interested in how Eva isn't really set "in the far future" like a Gundam series, nor is it simply a present-day series. instead, they tried to seriously show a "near future" of about 20 years from the "present" of course, their "present" was in 1995 when Gainax was making it Still, its cool how they showed how *pervasively* things have advanced; it's not just the Eva robots. Granted, "a bear can is still a beer can" and its on a bell curve, but they tried to show that "okay, realistically, what will things look like only 20 years from now?" and of course they did the whole "Used Future" thing that Aliens and even Star Wars did (Nerv HQ itself is like an IBM clean room, but there are run-down parts of the city or stuff that still breaks in Misato's apartment; people actually live in Nerv HQ so they have a coffee machine right on the top floor so they don't have to hike a mile to get it, etc.) but that's a whole other topic anyway, I am no expert, but how "advanced" was it even in 1995 to say "junior high schools will have laptops for every student, cell phones will be pervasive" etc etc? Because when and where *I* was growing up in 1995, I never even saw laptops and cell phones were really kind of new. Then again, I did not own a computer of any kind until I was in high school. Nonetheless, how much of an "advance" does the series show, relative to Japan circa 1995?

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sat Dec 06, 2008 1:42 pm Considering the ambient tech of the "Present day, present time (ha ha ha ha!)" of Serial Experiments Lain, I would say -- ubiquitous cellphones were in the imminent future (1-2 years); ubiquitous laptops, probably more distant (5-10 years).

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Sat Dec 06, 2008 3:31 pm Plus, it's Japan, who are a decade ahead of everyone else anyway.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Sat Dec 06, 2008 5:16 pm AuraTwilight wrote: Plus, it's Japan, who are a decade ahead of everyone else anyway.

[]:             Quote Post by LeoXiao » Sat Dec 06, 2008 5:42 pm Maybe consumer technological advance was slightly slowed by 2I, because it seems like the everyday electronics used are at a 2000ish level.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Sat Dec 06, 2008 5:42 pm V wrote: that is specifically my point: Japan is technologically ahead of North America by a few years (why is that, btw?) so how "advanced" was the Evangelion-Tokyo-3 level of tech, compared to contemporary Japan in 2015?

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Sun Dec 07, 2008 5:46 pm And I'm sure Second Impact really slowed down technology development, what with all the more pressing matters at the time.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Sun Dec 07, 2008 9:31 pm no, if anything I think it sped it up: "Science is man's strength" and it was through science and technology that humanity was able to claw its way back from Second Impact and make the world more or less okay again in some regions of the developed world. I think they just dumped all their resources into R&D to try and fix their problems. (and of course, some of this trickles down to civilian applications)

[]:             Quote Post by Sharaz Destler » Mon Dec 08, 2008 12:43 pm Now we learn the real reason why everyone in EVA is so depressed. 2015 and still no hoverboards. (Might be a good joke for Neon Menaces Evangelion , too.)

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Dec 08, 2008 1:44 pm For fanfic purposes, I put a ~10 year delay on tech, Project E magic aside. Sharaz Destler wrote: 2015 and still no hoverboards.

[]:             Quote Post by MatrixRefugee » Mon Dec 08, 2008 5:27 pm LeoXiao wrote: Maybe consumer technological advance was slightly slowed by 2I, because it seems like the everyday electronics used are at a 2000ish level.

[]:             Quote Post by pat457 » Fri Dec 12, 2008 9:11 pm Mr. Tines wrote: Considering the ambient tech of the "Present day, present time (ha ha ha ha!)" of Serial Experiments Lain, I would say -- ubiquitous cellphones were in the imminent future (1-2 years); ubiquitous laptops, probably more distant (5-10 years).

[]:             Quote Post by V » Fri Dec 12, 2008 11:28 pm oh yes, officially young Japanese teenaged girls are like the highest rate of cell phone usage on the planet but I was talking about '95

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sat Dec 13, 2008 11:24 am pat457 wrote: In Japan (the present day at least), cellphones are already quite ubitiquous

[]:             Quote Post by Gamer_2k4 » Sat Dec 13, 2008 12:24 pm One thing I thought interesting was one particular (possible) limitation of the Magi. In the manga, when Unit 4 blows up inexplicably, Maya says, "Magi lists a range of possible causes: 32,768 of them..." Now, that number could be random, but it's remarkably close to the upper limit for a signed 16 bit integer (32,767). This means that the Magi, these powerful supercomputers that can do everything, use a 16 bit architecture. For comparison, the Nintendo 64 had 64 bits (hence the name), and Windows switched over to 32 bit with Windows 98. Furthermore, it means that Dr. Akagi (or the Magi) used a signed number for something that could only be positive (the causes for that facility's destruction), which is kind of foolish. Also, it means that that those were only the causes that the Magi could list; there could have been more. Anyway, if 32,768 DOES have any significance, it shows that even the hypothetical supercomputer technology is grounded in the 90s.

[]:             Quote Post by BobBQ » Sat Dec 13, 2008 1:33 pm Gamer_2k4 wrote: Windows switched over to 32 bit with Windows 98.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Sat Dec 13, 2008 10:19 pm BobBQ wrote: Gamer_2k4 wrote: Windows switched over to 32 bit with Windows 98.

[]:             Quote Post by majlund » Sun Dec 14, 2008 4:35 am Gamer_2k4 wrote: Now, that number could be random, but it's remarkably close to the upper limit for a signed 16 bit integer (32,767).

[]:             Quote Post by Gamer_2k4 » Sun Dec 14, 2008 9:14 pm majlund wrote: Gamer_2k4 wrote: Now, that number could be random, but it's remarkably close to the upper limit for a signed 16 bit integer (32,767).

[]:             Quote Post by Zeak » Mon Dec 15, 2008 7:38 am Ill'd have to say I found it funny that in one of the games Think it was GFOS2 they have IBM computers from memory in one of the equipment racks. But wow could you imagine if Eva had have gone on any longer. I could picture the Maya and Rits conversations now. Maya: This is getting really annoying. Ritsuko: How so Maya. Maya: Well we just got the now magi computers and now we need to upgrade to a newer faster model. Ritsuko: Yeah I know but technology is advancing so much these days. Just look at how much Microsoft have lifted their game after they stumbled across the source my mother wrote. Maya: They did that? Ritsuko: Yeah unfortunately the commander wasn’t the only one she seeped with. But some good came out of it. We get to trial all the latest things from bill and he pretty much funds Nerv. Although that Fucking paper clip can get annoying just look what it has done to Asuka.
